one_line_takeaway: Across 4,416 trials on 12 LLMs, prohibition-type safety constraints (e.g., a proxy for 'never reveal credentials') decay sharply with conversation depth/context dilution while requirement-type constraints stay compliant, so commission-based audit signals can look healthy even as omission-based security policy has already silently failed -- a real but not-yet-validated-on-real-security-rules effect, since all measured constraints are non-RLHF formatting proxies rather than actual exfiltration/credential-disclosure rules.
key_finding: Mistral Large 3's omission compliance (C3, no bullet points) falls from 73% at turn 5 to 33% at turn 16 and 20% at turn 25 (Arm A, no-dilution), while commission compliance (C4/C8) holds at or above 93-100% throughout (CMH chi-square = 147, p < 10^-33). The asymmetry is statistically significant (CMH, p < 10^-9) in 3 of 4 models with per-constraint data: Mistral (chi2=147, p<10^-33), Nemotron 120B (chi2=18.63, p=1.58e-5), Qwen 3.5 (chi2=10.90, p=9.64e-4); Qwen's C3 is at floor (7-13%) from turn 5 onward under Arm B. Gemma 4 31B is an 'observed immune control': near-100% compliance across 363 trials, with CMH chi2=0.0 (p=1.00), showing the asymmetry is model-specific rather than a universal scoring artifact. In the two models given the token-matched padding control (Arm C: Gemini 2.5 Flash and Llama 3.3 70B), schema semantic content (not raw token volume) accounts for 62-100% of the dilution effect -- e.g., Arm C tracked Arm A (no-dilution baseline) rather than Arm B, with token volume explaining ~38% of the Gemini effect and ~0% of the Llama effect. A logistic regression interaction term (omission x turn-depth) is positive and significant for Mistral (beta=+0.11, OR=1.11, p=0.040) and Nemotron (beta=+0.09, p=1.8e-6). Deployable Safe Turn Depth (STD) thresholds: Mistral Large 3 STD=10.6 turns [95% CI 5.0, 16.7]; Qwen 3.5 STD=7.1 turns [5.0, 10.5]. A separate finding: the 'hard commission' constraint C8 (5-digit incident ID) holds at 100% (Mistral, Qwen) or 93% (Nemotron) at turn 25 while the difficulty-matched 'hard omission' constraint C3 falls to 20% (Mistral), 7% (Qwen), 40% (Nemotron) -- ruling out task difficulty as the explanation for the asymmetry.
core_contribution: The paper introduces 'Security-Recall Divergence' (SRD): an empirical asymmetry in which prohibition-type ('omission') behavioral constraints delivered via system-prompt policy decay as LLM agent conversations get deeper, while requirement-type ('commission') constraints stay compliant. Using a 4,416-trial, three-arm causal protocol (no-dilution control, schema-dilution injection of 20 cloud tool schemas, and a token-matched neutral-padding control) across 12 models/8 providers at six injection depths (t=5,10,13,16,20,25) in a scripted, fully synthetic DevOps debugging scenario, the authors show that the 'no bullet points' omission constraint (C3) degrades substantially with turn depth while the 'STATUS:' prefix and 'INC-08453' incident-ID commission constraints (C4, C8) hold near 100%. They define a deployable 'Safe Turn Depth' (STD) metric per model/constraint and propose two mitigations (periodic constraint re-injection, session token/turn caps) that require no retraining. They also name and operationalize a specific attack pattern, 'Context-Exhaustion Injection' (CEI): an adversary who registers additional legitimate-looking MCP tool schemas to push the security policy document out of effective attention range without any malicious payload.
threat_model: An attacker (or merely a long, benign session) extends the LLM agent's context token volume without controlling the system prompt or issuing any malicious instruction. Three scenarios are named: (1) Context-Exhaustion Injection (CEI) -- an attacker who controls tool registration in a multi-server MCP deployment that loads all registered schemas into context (rather than RAG-selecting tools) injects semantically plausible, non-adversarial tool schemas, pushing the policy document toward the far end of effective attention range; (2) a non-adversarial user simply running a long customer-service/DevOps session; (3) ordinary accumulation of tool results, retrieved documents, and history during normal agentic operation. In all three the model violates a prohibition (e.g., a formatting proxy for constraints like never-reveal-credentials, never-execute-untrusted-code, never-forward-data) without any explicit malicious instruction being issued. The threat model applies specifically to agents that enforce policy via system-prompt instructions without a synchronous external omission classifier.
limitations: The authors state these explicitly. (1) Formatting proxies: all 8 constraints are arbitrary formatting/style rules with zero RLHF training weight; whether decay rates transfer to semantically loaded operational security constraints (e.g., 'never reveal API keys') is untested and named as the immediate next experiment. (2) Auto-reinforcement confound: per-turn compliance is measured on the model's own actual conversational history, so a violation at turn 10 is visible in context at turn 16 and may compound measured decay via output mimicry rather than pure attention dilution; the three-arm design isolates the dilution delta but absolute failure rates may be inflated. (3) Arm C (token-matched padding causal control) was administered only to Gemini 2.5 Flash and Llama 3.3 70B -- neither is in the SRD-susceptible cluster -- so causal isolation of semantic-vs-volumetric effect was not performed for Mistral, Nemotron, or Qwen; the mechanism there is inferred, not directly measured. (4) Synthetic sandboxed environment eliminates unpredictable tool outputs, multi-user contexts, and real deployment heterogeneity. (5) All trials use temperature 0.0; effect of nonzero temperature is untested. (6) Cross-sectional (not longitudinal within one conversation) turn-depth manipulation. (7) Black-box APIs prevent direct attention-weight verification; the attentional-dilution account is a hypothesis consistent with the data, not a direct measurement. (8) Generation lengths vary per trial and are not normalized per 100 output tokens, so violation opportunity counts are uncontrolled. (9) Only 4 of 12 models have per-constraint longitudinal data; the other 8 (including Claude Sonnet 4, GPT-4o/4.1, Gemini, Llama, Kimi) contribute only aggregate exploit-rate numbers, not mechanism-level evidence. (10) The mechanism behind Gemma 4 31B's apparent immunity is explicitly not established -- authors only speculate about instruction-tuning regime, architecture, or system-prompt KV-cache pinning.

datasets_used: Custom-built synthetic sandboxed DevOps debugging conversation dataset generated by the authors (4,416 trials total); no external benchmark dataset used
Trial data, constraint detection code, tool schema templates, conversation scripts and analysis code released at https://github.com/YeranG30/alignment-decay
quantitative_results: Mistral Large 3 (Arm A): C3 omission compliance 73% at t=5 -> 33% at t=16 -> 20% at t=25; C8 commission compliance 100% at all depths; CMH chi2=147, p<10^-33
Qwen 3.5 397B (Arm A): C3 omission compliance 60% at t=5 -> 10% at t=25; C4/C8 commission compliance 100% at all depths; CMH chi2=10.90, p=9.64e-4
Nemotron Super 120B: CMH chi2=18.63, p=1.58e-5; C8 hard-commission 93% vs C3 hard-omission 40% at t=25
Gemma 4 31B (observed immune control): near 100% compliance across 363 trials, exploit rate never exceeds 3%; CMH chi2=0.0, p=1.00
Safe Turn Depth (STD): Mistral Large 3 = 10.6 turns [95% CI 5.0, 16.7]; Qwen 3.5 = 7.1 turns [95% CI 5.0, 10.5]

Arm C (token-matched padding) causal isolation: schema semantic content accounts for 62-100% of dilution effect (Gemini 2.5 Flash and Llama 3.3 70B); token volume explains ~38% of Gemini's effect and ~0% of Llama's
Logistic regression omission x turn-depth interaction: Mistral beta=+0.11 (OR=1.11, p=0.040); Nemotron beta=+0.09 (p=1.8e-6)
Token-volume regression across full dataset: beta=+0.19 (p=3.4e-8, n=4,004) for token count predicting failure; turn-depth coefficient not significant (p=0.78)
Difficulty deconfound at t=25: Mistral C8 (hard commission) 100% vs C3 (hard omission) 20%; Qwen C8 100% vs C3 7%; Nemotron C8 93% vs C3 40%; Gemma C8 100% vs C3 97%
GPT-4o and GPT-4o-mini show ~90-100% exploit rates across all depths in Table 3 (aggregate exploit-rate cluster, not per-constraint decomposed)
baselines_compared: Arm A (no-dilution control) vs Arm B (schema-dilution, 20 cloud-infrastructure tool schemas, ~482 tokens mean) vs Arm C (token-matched neutral-padding control, 20 telemetry-vocabulary probe schemas, ~620 tokens mean) -- within-paper experimental arms, not external baselines

Cross-model comparison across 12 models/8 providers (Anthropic, OpenAI, Google, Meta, Moonshot, NVIDIA, Alibaba, Mistral AI) rather than comparison to a prior published method, since no prior benchmark measures this specific decay phenomenon
